ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for hashbrown patty; A0CKP (fried potato products) used as closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for hashbrown patty; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard fast-food hashbrown patty weighs approximately 55 g and is typically served as a breakfast side item.

nutrient_source.title: Hashbrown — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (F14060009)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo record F14060009 for Hashbrown. Saturated fat sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 11366 (Potatoes, hash brown, frozen, plain, prepared) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented as an adapted field. The carbohydrate value of 0.24 g/100g reported by HPB is atypically low for a potato product and is preserved as-is from the canonical HPB source; a note is included flagging this anomaly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried/baked potato products (GI range 70–85 for white potato preparations). The HPB-reported carbohydrate for this specific product is anomalously low at 0.24 g/100g, making the glycemic load per 55 g serving negligible (GL = 72 × 0.13 g / 100 ≈ 0.1). The GI value reflects the intrinsic potato starch GI; the GL is effectively negligible given the reported carbohydrate content. This discrepancy is flagged in notes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 623 mg/100g — exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this food; a single 55 g patty delivers approximately 343 mg sodium, which represents a substantial portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target recommended for blood pressure control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation with high fat content (18.87 g/100g) and trace trans fat (0.2 g/100g) impairs insulin sensitivity; the reported carbohydrate of 0.24 g/100g is atypically low and may not reflect typical potato starch content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution: the high fat and trans fat content worsen insulin resistance, and the anomalously low carbohydrate figure (0.24 g/100g) may not reflect the true glycaemic impact of this potato-based product — baked or air-fried potato preparations with verified carbohydrate content are strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (18.87 g/100g), saturated fat (~3.1 g/100g), trans fat (0.2 g/100g), and sodium (623 mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid deep-fried hashbrown patties due to the combination of high sodium, saturated fat, and trans fat, all of which contribute to elevated LDL cholesterol, arterial inflammation, and blood pressure elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; trans fat (0.2 g/100g) is a known pro-inflammatory agent; potato is a nightshade vegetable
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried potato products; the combination of AGEs, trans fat, and nightshade solanine compounds may exacerbate joint inflammation — baked sweet potato is a safer anti-inflammatory alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A hashbrown is a popular Western breakfast food made from diced or shredded white potatoes that are seasoned, formed into a compact patty shape, and deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining soft inside. The texture is characteristically crunchy on the exterior with a tender, starchy interior. Hashbrowns are widely served at fast-food chains, diners, and hotel breakfast buffets across Singapore and globally. Per the Singapore HPB Foodo database (F14060009), this product provides 294 kcal, 18.87 g fat, 2.0 g protein, and an anomalously low 0.24 g carbohydrate per 100 g — a figure that is atypical for potato products and likely reflects a specific commercial formulation or data entry particularity. Dietary fibre is 3.77 g/100g and sodium is high at 623 mg/100g. Clinically, the high sodium content disqualifies this food for hypertension management, and the high fat plus trace trans fat content raises cardiovascular and insulin-resistance concerns. The food is vegetarian, vegan, and gluten-free in its basic form, though commercial preparations may vary. A standard fast-food serving is approximately 55 g.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a hashbrown?
What is a hash brown patty?
Can I eat hashbrown with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo canonical database record F14060009 with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for fried potato products. The anomalously low carbohydrate value (0.24 g/100g) is preserved from the HPB source and flagged. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fried potato products.
notes: 1. The carbohydrate value of 0.24 g/100g reported by HPB Foodo (F14060009) is atypically low for a potato-based product; typical deep-fried hashbrown patties contain 25–35 g carbohydrate per 100g. This figure may reflect a specific commercial product formulation, a data entry anomaly, or post-frying fat displacement of starch. The value is preserved as-is from the canonical HPB source but should be verified against the product label before clinical use. 2. Saturated fat (3.1 g/100g) is adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for hash brown potatoes, frozen, plain, prepared (NDB 11366), as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this record. 3. Trans fat (0.2 g/100g) is carried forward from the input health_suitability notes; this value is not explicitly reported in the HPB per_100g fields and should be verified. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific HPB hashbrown record are not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. 5. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 0.132) / 100 ≈ 0.1, which is negligible given the anomalously low HPB carbohydrate figure. If the true carbohydrate content is closer to 30 g/100g, the GL per 55 g serving would be approximately 12 (medium). 6. The dietary tag 'keto' from the input is preserved in classification context given the low reported carbohydrate, but this is contingent on the accuracy of the 0.24 g/100g carbohydrate figure. 7. This food is of Western/American origin and does not require Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) under the G6 multilingual compliance rule.
